curveRfreq 0.0.0.9000
  • Reference
    • Comparison of five nonlinear regression models for standard curve fitting
    • Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: ELISA Example
    • Frequentist Modeling of Immunoassay Standard Curves with the StandardCurve Class: Bead Array Example

Comparison of five nonlinear regression models for standard curve fitting

Source: vignettes/model_comparisons.Rmd
model_comparisons.Rmd

Consider the five nonlinear models used in the curveRfreq package with concentration as the predictor. Writing xx for concentration:

  1. logistic4: 4-parameter logistic in xx

y∼a+d−a1+exp(x−cb) y \sim a + \frac{d - a}{1 + \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)}

  1. log-logistic4: 4-parameter log-logistic (Hill-type)

y∼a+d−a1+(xc)b y \sim a + \frac{d - a}{1 + \left(\frac{x}{c}\right)^b}

  1. gompertz4: 4-parameter Gompertz-type in xx

y∼a+(d−a)exp(−exp(−b(x−c))) y \sim a + (d - a)\exp\left(-\exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)\right)

  1. logistic5: 5-parameter generalized logistic (asymmetric)

y∼d+a−d(1+exp(x−cb))g y \sim d + \frac{a - d}{\left(1 + \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)\right)^g}

  1. loglogistic5: 5-parameter generalized log-logistic (asymmetric)

y∼a+(d−a)(1+gexp(−b(x−c)))−1/g y \sim a + (d - a)\left(1 + g\exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)\right)^{-1/g}

We compare their forms, how they fit assay response vs. concentration, and their performance and numerical stability.

1. logistic4: 4-Parameter logistic model in concentration (x)

Model form

logistic4

y(x)=a+d−a1+exp(x−cb) y(x) = a + \frac{d - a}{1 + \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)}

Parameters

  • aa: lower asymptote (response at very low xx)
  • dd: upper asymptote (response at very high xx)
  • cc: inflection point in xx (the xx value where the response is halfway between aa and dd)
  • bb: horizontal scale (slope) parameter on the linear xx scale

Transform and symmetry

Rearranging:

y−ad−y=exp(x−cb) \frac{y - a}{d - y} = \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)

Taking logarithms:

ln(y−ad−y)=x−cb \ln\left(\frac{y - a}{d - y}\right) = \frac{x - c}{b}

So this transformed response is linear in xx, and the curve is:

  • Symmetric (S-shaped) around x=cx = c on the original xx axis.
  • bb controls how quickly the response transitions around cc.

The response at the inflection point is:

yinflection=a+d2 y_{\text{inflection}} = \frac{a + d}{2}

Fit behavior, performance, and stability

  • Works well when the S-shape of yy vs. xx is roughly symmetric in xx.
  • Easy to interpret and typically numerically stable with reasonable starting values.
  • May be less appropriate if concentration spans orders of magnitude and the natural symmetry is on a log scale, or if the empirical curve is clearly asymmetric.

2. log-logistic4: 4-parmameter log-logistic (Hill) model

Model form

loglogistic4

y(x)=a+d−a1+(xc)by(x) = a + \frac{d - a}{1 + \left(\frac{x}{c}\right)^b}

Parameters

  • aa: lower asymptote
  • dd: upper asymptote
  • cc: inflection point (the xx value where yy is halfway between aa and dd)
  • bb: Hill slope parameter acting on a logarithmic xx scale## Transform and symmetry

Rearranging: d−yy−a=(xc)b\frac{d - y}{y - a} = \left(\frac{x}{c}\right)^b

Taking logarithms:

ln(d−yy−a)=b[ln(x)−ln(c)]\ln\left(\frac{d - y}{y - a}\right)=b \left[\ln(x) - \ln(c)\right]

So the transformed response is linear in ln(x)\ln(x). The curve is:

  • Symmetric when plotted against ln(x)\ln(x) (or log10(x)\log_{10}(x)).
  • bb controls how steeply the response changes per unit change in ln(x)\ln(x).
  • The response at the inflection point is:yinflection=a+d2y_{\text{inflection}} = \frac{a + d}{2}

Fit behavior, performance, and stability

  • Well suited for dose–response relationships where xx spans orders of magnitude and the curve looks symmetric on a log(concentration) axis.

  • Parameters have clear interpretations:

    • aa and dd are asymptotes,
    • cc is the EC50-type midpoint,
    • bb is the Hill slope.

  • Numerically robust in typical dose–response applications with multiplicative concentration steps (for example, 0.1,1,10,1000.1, 1, 10, 100).

  • The intended log-scale interpretation breaks down if xx is near zero or negative, though the function can still be fitted algebraically when x>0x > 0.

3. gompertz4: 4-parameter Gompertz-type model in x

Model Form

gompertz4

y(x)=a+(d−a)exp(−exp(−b(x−c))) y(x) = a + (d - a)\exp\left(-\exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)\right)

Parameters

  • aa: lower asymptote
  • dd: upper asymptote as xx becomes large
  • cc: location parameter associated with the inflection region
  • bb: growth/slope parameter on the linear xx scale

Shape and asymmetry

Define:

G(x)=exp(−exp(−b(x−c))) G(x) = \exp\left(-\exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)\right)

Then:

y(x)=a+(d−a)G(x) y(x) = a + (d - a)G(x)

where:

  • G(x)G(x) ranges from 00 to 11.
  • For very low xx,

exp(−b(x−c)) \exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)

is large, so:

G(x)≈exp(−large)≈0 G(x) \approx \exp(-\text{large}) \approx 0

  • For very high xx,

−b(x−c) -b(x - c)

is very negative, giving:

exp(−b(x−c))≈0 \exp\left(-b(x - c)\right) \approx 0

and therefore:

G(x)≈exp(0)=1 G(x) \approx \exp(0) = 1

The response at the inflection point is:

yinflection=a+(d−a)e−1 y_{\text{inflection}} = a + (d - a)e^{-1}

Curve characteristics

The Gompertz curve is:

  • Inherently asymmetric in xx.
  • Typically rises relatively quickly from the lower tail and then approaches the upper asymptote more gradually (or vice versa depending on sign conventions).

Fit behavior, performance, and stability

  • Useful when data clearly show asymmetric sigmoidal behavior that logistic or log-logistic forms do not capture well.
  • Provides flexibility particularly in how the upper tail approaches dd.
  • Parameter interpretation (especially cc and bb) is generally less intuitive than for logistic/Hill-type models.
  • Numerically more delicate because of nested exponentials; very large values of

|b(x−c)| |b(x - c)|

can cause underflow or overflow in exponential terms and may require constrained fitting or careful starting values.

4. logistic5: 5-parameter generalized logistic model in xx (asymmetric logistic)

Model form

logistic5

y(x)=d+a−d(1+exp(x−cb))g y(x) = d + \frac{a - d} {\left(1 + \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)\right)^g}

It is convenient to rewrite the model as:

y(x)=a+(d−a)(1+exp(x−cb))−g y(x) = a + (d - a) \left(1 + \exp\left(\frac{x - c}{b}\right)\right)^{-g}

Parameters

  • aa: lower asymptote
  • dd: upper asymptote
  • cc: location parameter analogous to a center or inflection-like position in xx
  • bb: scale/slope parameter on the linear xx scale
  • gg: asymmetry (shape) parameter

The inflection location is:

xinflection=c+blog(g) x_{\text{inflection}} = c + b \log(g)

The response at the inflection point is:

yinflection=d+a−d(1+g)g y_{\text{inflection}} = d + \frac{a - d}{(1 + g)^g}

When:

g=1 g = 1

the logistic4 model reduces to the standard 4-parameter logistic model (up to the aa–dd sign convention). Thus, logistic5 generalizes logistic4 by introducing an additional shape parameter gg that allows asymmetric sigmoidal forms on the linear xx scale.

Shape and asymmetry

  • For g≠1g \neq 1, the left and right shoulders of the sigmoid differ in steepness and extent; the curve is no longer symmetric about x=cx = c.
  • Values of g>1g > 1 or g<1g < 1 stretch or compress one side of the curve relative to the other.
  • As: g→1g \to 1 the model approaches the symmetric logistic-in-xx behavior.

Fit behavior

  • The logistic5 moodel can capture asymmetric sigmoidal relationships in xx while retaining logistic-like tails and a logistic-like central transition region.
  • This is useful when the symmetric logistic4 model misfits one tail but otherwise captures the overall S-shaped trend.

Performance and stability

  • More flexible than logistic4 because of the additional asymmetry parameter gg, but also more complex to fit.
  • Increased flexibility can improve empirical fit quality but may also introduce parameter trade-offs and stronger parameter correlation (particularly among bb, cc, and gg).
  • These dependencies can reduce numerical stability or lead to near-nonidentifiability in some datasets.
  • Good starting values (often initializing with g≈1g \approx 1) and parameter bounds are often important for stable estimation and to avoid degenerate curve shapes.

loglogistic5: 5-parameter generalized log-logistic model (asymmetric)

Model form

loglogistic5

y(x)=a+(d−a)(1+gexp(−b(x−c)))−1/g y(x) = a + (d - a) \left( 1 + g \exp\left(-b(x - c)\right) \right)^{-1/g}

Parameters

  • aa: lower asymptote
  • dd: upper asymptote
  • cc: location parameter on the xx scale
  • bb: scale/slope parameter, similar to a logistic slope factor in xx
  • gg: shape/asymmetry parameter

The inflection location is:

xinflection=c+log(g)b x_{\text{inflection}} = c + \frac{\log(g)}{b}

The response at the inflection point is:

yinflection=a+(d−a)(1+g)−1/g y_{\text{inflection}} = a + (d - a)(1 + g)^{-1/g}

For small values of gg,

g→0 g \to 0

the model approaches an exponential-type logistic form. For nonzero gg, the model defines a generalized family that includes several asymmetric sigmoidal shapes and is closely related to generalized logistic families and certain reparameterizations of Gompertz and log-logistic models.

Shape and asymmetry

  • The curve is sigmoidal between aa and dd, while allowing flexible asymmetric transition behavior.
  • The parameter gg explicitly modulates curvature and asymmetry of the S-curve.
  • Depending on the sign and magnitude of gg, one tail may become longer, flatter, or steeper relative to the other.

Relation to log-scale interpretations

Although the model contains the exponential term:

exp(−b(x−c)) \exp\left(-b(x - c)\right)

the resulting model is not the standard symmetric log-logistic form. Instead, it represents a generalized asymmetric family capable of approximating:

  • Logistic-type curves,
  • Gompertz-like curves,
  • Other asymmetric sigmoidal shapes.

Thus, loglogistic5 provides substantially greater flexibility than the symmetric loglogistic4 model.

Fit behavior, performance, and stability

  • Potentially the most flexible of the five models for describing asymmetric S-shaped dose–response relationships in xx.
  • The additional flexibility comes with increased risk of overfitting and stronger parameter correlations, particularly among bb, cc, and gg, and in interaction with asymptotes aa and dd.
  • Numerical fitting may be less stable when data do not strongly constrain both tails and the central transition region.
  • Good starting values and reasonable parameter bounds for gg are often important for stable optimization.
  • Interpretation of gg is more abstract than the primary logistic parameters, since it primarily governs asymmetry and tail behavior rather than a directly observable biological quantity.

6. Comparative Summary of all five models

(a) Symmetry, scale, and shape

  • logistic4 (4-parameter logistic in x)

    • Symmetric S-curve on the linear x scale

    • Appropriate when the empirical curve is roughly symmetric in x

  • loglogistic4 (4-parameter log-logistic)

    • Symmetric S-curve on a log(concentration)log(concentration) scale.

    • Appropriate for standard dose–response data spanning orders of magnitude, where plotting vs. log(x)log(x) gives a symmetric S.

    • gompertz4 (Gompertz-type in xx)

      • Intrinsically asymmetric on the xx scale.
      • Good when one tail is clearly more gradual or more abrupt than the other.

  • logistic5 (5-parameter generalized logistic in xx):

    • Logistic-like but with an additional shape parameter gg to control asymmetry in xx.
    • Bridges between symmetric logistic behavior (g=1)(g = 1) and a family of asymmetric logistic-like curves.

  • loglogistic5 (5-parameter generalized log-logistic):

    • Generalized asymmetric sigmoidal form with rich shape control via g.
    • Can mimic or approximate various asymmetric logistic/Gompertz-type shapes.

(b) Interpretation of slope and shape parameters

  • logistic4

    • bb is a horizontal slope/scale in units of xx; cc is the inflection point.

  • loglogistic4

    • bb is a Hill slope on the log scale; cc is the inflection point; shape is symmetric in ln(x)ln(x).

  • gompertz4

    • bb controls growth and decay in a nested exponential; cc controls location of the rapid-growth region; no simple “symmetric slope” interpretation.

  • logistic5

  • bb still controls global steepness in xx, but gg adjusts asymmetry of the S-curve around cc.

  • When g≈1g \approx 1), it behaves like logistic4; deviations from 1 tilt/stretch the curve’s shoulders.

  • loglogistic5

    • b affects how quickly the transition occurs as xx passes cc; gg fine-tunes the asymmetry and tail behavior of the curve built from exp(−b*(x−c))exp(-b * (x - c)).

  • Interpretation is more qualitative (shape control) than a simple single-slope concept.

(c) Fit behavior across wide concentration ranges

  • logistic4

    • Best when data are reasonably symmetric on a linear xx axis, and xx does not span extremely wide ranges.

  • loglogistic4

    • Typically best for classical dose–response (e.g., pharmacology, toxicology) where log10(x)log_{10}(x) spacing is used; symmetry in log-space.

  • gompertz4

    • Useful when the approach to the upper plateau (or the rise from the lower plateau) is clearly more stretched than the other tail and simpler logistic forms cannot capture this.

  • logistic5

    • Adds asymmetry to the logistic-in-x family, improving fit where logisic4 is too rigid but the Gompertz-type gompertz4 does not match the central region well.

  • Good when the overall S-shape is logistic-like but visibly skewed

  • loglogistic5

  • The most flexible of the “logistic / log-logistic-like” families considered here.

  • Can capture nuanced asymmetries and tail behaviors over wide xx ranges, particularly useful when both tails and the center are well-sampled and clearly deviate from standard logistic or Hill forms.

(d) Performance and numerical stability

  • logistic4
    • Simple and generally stable; minimal risk of over-parameterization.
    • May misfit asymmetric curves.
  • loglogistic4
    • Similarly stable and widely used; excellent for typical log-spaced dose–response data.
    • Interpretation of parameters is straightforward and biologically meaningful in many contexts.
    • Use when concentrations span orders of magnitude and the curve is symmetric in log(x)log(x); this is often the default for classical dose–response analysis.
  • gompertz4
    • More flexible for asymmetry but can be numerically sensitive due to nested exponentials; large values of |b*(x−c)||b * (x - c)| can cause extreme function values.
    • Requires careful starting values and potentially parameter constraints.
  • logistic5
    • More flexible than logistic4 but with an extra parameter gg, which may be weakly identified if data do not strongly exhibit asymmetry.
    • Potential for increased correlation between parameters (b,c,g)(b, c, g), leading to less stable fits if data are sparse or noisy in either tail.
  • loglogistic5
    • Highest flexibility and highest parameter count among the five.
    • Can deliver superior fits on complex asymmetric data, but has the greatest risk of:
      • Overfitting,
      • Poor identifiability of gg,
      • Numerical instability without good initial values and appropriate bounds.
      • Interpretation focuses on qualitative shape rather than simple inflection point and slope.
    • Interpretation focuses on qualitative shape rather than simple inflection point and slope.
